title: Cedar Ecology and the Cedar Teaching
description: Learn to identify western red cedar and understand its place in PNW Coast traditions. Explore the Cedar Teaching — the origin story of Grandmother Cedar / Xpey' — and the Law of the Bark as the ethical foundation of all cedar use.

steps.instruction: Learn to identify western red cedar by its four diagnostic features: scale-like overlapping leaves that lie flat like braided rope, fibrous reddish-brown bark that strips vertically from the trunk, strongly buttressed base that flares out above the roots, and aromatic reddish-brown heartwood that smells distinctively spicy when fresh-cut.
steps.culturalContext: Cedar recognition is the beginning of all cedar knowledge. Coast Salish, Nuu-chah-nulth, Kwakwaka'wakw, and Makah peoples have each developed detailed ecological knowledge of cedar — where it grows best, how it changes through seasons, what it signals about soil moisture and forest health. Cedar identification is not a field skill separate from cultural knowledge; it is part of the same body of knowledge. When you can identify cedar reliably, you are beginning to learn what these four nations have always known.

steps.instruction: Study the Cedar Teaching: the origin story of Grandmother Cedar — Xpey' in Lekwungen and Coast Salish languages.
steps.culturalContext: The story of the man who gave everything away — who was transformed by the Creator into cedar as a continuing gift to the people — is widely shared in community-authorized educational materials. Grandmother Cedar is not metaphor but relationship. The man who gave everything is still giving: bark for weaving, wood for canoes, planks for longhouses, roots for baskets, rope for lashing. This is Level 1 publicly shared knowledge. Specific ceremony protocols associated with beginning cedar work are Level 3-4 and are not reproduced here. To work with cedar is to continue receiving the gift that Grandmother Cedar represents — and the Law of the Bark is how that gift is received with respect.

steps.instruction: Learn the Law of the Bark: the ethical framework for cedar harvesting that encodes both ecological wisdom and relational ethics.
steps.culturalContext: The Law of the Bark has five core elements: ask permission before beginning (the specific forms of asking are Level 3-4; the practice of asking is Level 1), take only what you need for the work at hand, never strip more than one-third of a tree's circumference in a single harvest (the one-third rule ensures the tree survives), close the wound when you are finished (re-covering exposed sapwood reduces infection and drying), and express gratitude (the specific forms of expression are Level 3-4; the practice of gratitude is Level 1). This is the Law of the Bark as ecological ethics encoded in practice — it is integral to all cedar content in this room, not optional background.

steps.instruction: Learn cedar's ecology: range, growth, climate threats, and sustainable harvest timing.
steps.culturalContext: Western red cedar (*Thuja plicata*) grows from northern California to southeast Alaska, primarily west of the Cascade crest at lower elevations. It reaches 60+ metres in ideal conditions over centuries. Climate change is affecting cedar across its range: increased summer drought causes crown dieback in eastern and southern portions, bark beetle pressure is rising, and warmer winters reduce the snowpack that feeds spring moisture. Traditional harvest timing — spring, when sap runs and inner bark separates cleanly from sapwood — reflects ecological knowledge that aligns with the tree's biology. Harvesting in fall or dry summer conditions damages the tree more severely because the bark tears rather than peeling cleanly.

steps.instruction: Identify what cedar provides: the full range of materials that western red cedar yields for Coast Salish, Nuu-chah-nulth, Kwakwaka'wakw, and Makah material culture.
steps.culturalContext: Cedar provides: inner bark (bast/phloem) for weaving — baskets, mats, cloaks, hats, rope, and cordage; outer bark for construction waterproofing and roofing; wood for canoes (dugout from single logs), bentwood boxes (kerfed and steam-bent single planks), longhouses (post-and-beam frames with split-plank cladding), totem poles, house posts, and masks; roots for fine basket weaving (spruce roots are also used in some traditions); and withes (flexible young branches) for additional lashing. Each of these materials requires different harvest techniques and seasonal timing — all governed by the Law of the Bark as the overarching framework.

steps.instruction: Reflect on the difference between SUMO's classification of cedar as 'Plant' and the understanding of cedar as Grandmother — Xpey' — in Coast Salish and related traditions.
steps.culturalContext: SUMO classifies western red cedar as Plant — a biological category based on its physical properties, cellular structure, and metabolic behavior. This is accurate: cedar is a plant, a conifer, a member of the cypress family (Cupressaceae). Coast Salish and related traditions understand cedar as Grandmother — Xpey' — a being whose generosity continues through every plank, basket, and rope. Both descriptions are true. They answer different questions: WHAT it is (biological classification) versus WHAT it means (relationship, gift, responsibility). A botanist who knows only the Plant classification can describe the tree accurately. A carver who understands Xpey' knows why the tree must be treated with care and gratitude long after the harvest is done. This room holds both views together — no pan-Indigenous generalization, no reduction to 'generic spirituality.' This teaching belongs specifically to Coast Salish, Nuu-chah-nulth, Kwakwaka'wakw, and Makah traditions.
